text:
*Baaji* (Urdu: باجی) is a 2019 Pakistani social drama film directed by Saqib Malik and Shah Fahad, and written by Irfan Ahmed Urfi. The film stars Meera, Osman Khalid Butt, Amna Ilyas, and Mohsin Abbas Haider. Set against the backdrop of the fading Lollywood film industry, *Baaji* explores themes of ambition, betrayal, and the struggle for relevance.

Produced by Page 33 Films and distributed by ARY Films, *Baaji* was released nationwide on June 28, 2019, and internationally by B4U Films. A teaser trailer was released in April 2019, followed by a theatrical trailer in May. The film generated significant buzz, further amplified by the release of a controversial song.

*Baaji* was a commercial success, grossing Rs. 120 million domestically against a budget of Rs. 60 million. It received generally positive reviews, with critics praising the performances of the cast, particularly Meera and Amna Ilyas. The film was later made available for streaming on Amazon Prime Video and had its television premiere on ARY Digital on Eid al-Fitr, May 26, 2020.

Daniel Berthiaume (born January 26, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey goaltender. Drafted by the Winnipeg Jets in 1985, he played in the NHL from 1986 to 1994, later playing in minor leagues and Europe until 2005.

Andre Royal (born December 1, 1972) is a former American football linebacker. He played college football at Alabama, where he was part of the 1992 National Championship team. Undrafted in 1995, he played professionally for the Carolina Panthers and Indianapolis Colts. Royal's career ended prematurely due to head injuries, though he was later offered a contract to return to the NFL. He completed his college degree in 2014.

The Federal Network Agency (BNetzA) is a German federal agency and regulatory office for electricity, gas, telecommunications, postal services, and railway infrastructure. It was formed in 1998 to regulate privatized infrastructure.

The 2001 South American U-20 Championship was an international youth football tournament. This article lists the squads of the participating teams, including Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Ecuador, Paraguay, Peru, Uruguay, and Venezuela.

George Stiebel (1821-1896) was a Jamaican trader and entrepreneur who became a millionaire with sea transport between North and South America and as the owner of a gold mine. Stiebel is notoriously named Jamaica's first black millionaire.
Timothy Greene (born 1969) is a South African actor, film director, and writer. He is known for his film *Boy Called Twist*, which pioneered a new approach to film finance in South Africa.

Paul Lawrence Smith (June 24, 1936 – April 25, 2012) was an American-Israeli actor. His first role was in *Exodus*, which was filmed in Israel. He appeared in *Midnight Express* (1978), *Popeye* (1980), and *Dune* (1984). Smith also appeared in television series such as *Emergency!*, *CHiPS*, and *Hawaii Five-O*. He died in Ra'anana, Israel, at age 75.

SMS Eber was the last of the six gunboats of the Iltis class of the German Imperial Navy prior to and during World War I. Other ships of the class are SMS Iltis, SMS Luchs, SMS Tiger, SMS Jaguar and SMS Panther. They were built between 1898 and 1903. All of them served primarily overseas, in the German colonies. Eber had a crew of 9 officers and 121 men.
**Luke Michael Haines** (born 7 October 1967) is an English singer-songwriter. He is known for being the frontman of the bands The Auteurs, Baader-Meinhof, and Black Box Recorder.
Barysaw (Lang-be, IPA-be) or Borisov (Lang-ru, IPA-ru) is a city in Belarus near the Berezina River in the Minsk Region 74 km north-east from Minsk. Its population is around 145,000.

Paul Lawrence Smith (June 24, 1936 – April 25, 2012) was an American-Israeli actor. Burly, bearded and imposing, he appeared in feature films and occasionally on television since the 1970s, generally playing "heavies" and bad guys. His most notable roles include Hamidou, the vicious prison guard in Midnight Express (1978), Bluto in Robert Altman's Popeye (1980), Gideon in the ABC miniseries Masada (1981), Glossu Rabban in David Lynch's Dune (1984) and Falkon in Red Sonja (1985).
Abderahmane Hachoud (born 1988) is an Algerian footballer who plays as a defender for MC Alger and the Algerian national team.

Howard John Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American baseball pitcher. He played professional baseball for 16 years from 1914 to 1930, including 15 seasons in Major League Baseball for the Buffalo Blues (1915), Detroit Tigers (1916–1917, 1919–1922), Boston Red Sox (1923–1926), and Philadelphia Athletics (1926–1930).

Ehmke compiled a career win–loss record of 166–166 with a 3.75 earned run average (ERA). His greatest success was with the Red Sox, including a no-hitter and his only 20-win season in 1923. Ehmke still holds the American League record for fewest hits allowed (one) in two consecutive starts. Ehmke also ranks sixteenth all-time in hitting batters. He hit 137 batters in his career and led the American League in the category seven times, including a career-high 23 in 1922. Ehmke is best known for being the surprise starter who won Game 1 of the 1929 World Series for the Athletics at the age of 35.

After retiring from baseball, he started his own company that began making tarpaulins to cover baseball diamonds during rain.

The Liberators'civil war (43–42 BC) was started by the Second Triumvirate to avenge Julius Caesar's assassination. The war was fought by the forces of Mark Antony and Octavian (the Second Triumvirate members) against the forces of Caesar's assassins, led by Marcus Junius Brutus and Gaius Cassius Longinus, also called the Liberatores. The latter were defeated by the Triumvirs at the Battle of Philippi in October 42 BC, and committed suicide. Brutus would also commit suicide after the second part of the battle.

The Ministry of Health (Turkish: Sağlık Bakanlığı) is a governmental agency of Turkey responsible for overseeing and regulating the country's healthcare system. It was founded on May 3, 1920, following the establishment of the Grand National Assembly. The ministry's initial focus was on addressing the aftermath of war and establishing healthcare legislation. Key periods in its history include the National Health Plan (1946-1960), health policies from 1960-2002, and the Health Transformation Program (2003-2013). The current Minister of Health is Fahrettin Koca, and the ministry employs over one million people.
Majha Hoshil Na (‹See RfD› transl.- Will You Be Mine?) is an Indian Marathi television drama that aired on Zee Marathi. The Story revolves around the Sai, Aditya and his Maternal uncles.

Half Man Half Biscuit are an English rock band, formed in 1984 in Birkenhead, Merseyside. Known for their satirical, sardonic, and sometimes surreal songs, the band comprises lead singer and guitarist Nigel Blackwell, bassist and singer Neil Crossley, drummer Carl Henry, and guitarist Karl Benson.

Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American engineer and religious leader who was an apostle in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) from 1918 to 1943.

Lyman is often noted as the most recent LDS Church apostle to have been excommunicated. In 1943, J. Reuben Clark, the first counselor in the LDS church's First Presidency and the church's de facto leader due to church president Heber J. Grant's poor health, became aware that Lyman had for a number of years been in an intimate relationship with a woman he had earlier been assigned to counsel. The Quorum of the Twelve Apostles convened a disciplinary council to hear the case and Lyman's explanation, after which he was immediately excommunicated. Lyman was rebaptized in 1954 at age 83, and his full priesthood blessings were restored posthumously in 1970.
The Colored Hockey League (CHL) was founded in 1895 in Nova Scotia, Canada by Black leaders to attract young Black men to Sunday worship. The league had teams in Nova Scotia and Prince Edward Island.

"Just Got Started Lovin' You" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music artist James Otto. It was released in July 2007 as the first single from his album Sunset Man. On the Hot Country Songs chart dated for May 17, 2008, the song has also become Otto's first (and to date, his only) number-one hit. The song was also the number one song on Billboard's year-end 2008 Hot Country Songs chart.

Amritsar–Jamnagar Expressway (NH-754) is an under-construction 1,257 km long, 4/6-lane wide expressway in the north-western part of India. The expressway will reduce the distance and time travel between Amritsar and Jamnagar from earlier 1,430 km to 1,316 km (including Kapurthala-Amritsar section) and the time travel from 26 hours to only 13 hours. It is a part of the Bharatmala and Amritsar–Jamnagar Economic Corridor (EC-3). It will pass through four states of Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan and Gujarat.

The expressway is strategically important, as it will connect 3 big oil refineries of HMEL Bathinda, HPCL Barmer and RIL Jamnagar. It will also connect Guru Nanak Dev Thermal Plant (Bathinda) and Suratgarh Super Thermal Power Plant (Sri Ganganagar).

The expressway will meet the Ludhiana-Bathinda-Ajmer Expressway of the Pathankot–Ajmer Economic Corridor at Bathinda. The construction work on the expressway started in Haryana and Rajasthan in 2019, and it is expected to be completed by September 2023.

The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was part of the 2017 NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision season. The season began on September 2, 2017, and concluded on December 2, 2017. Ten teams competed in the Big 12 Conference. Television partners included ABC, FOX, ESPN, FS1, FSN, and LHN.

Oklahoma was selected as the preseason favorite to win the conference. The season culminated in the Big 12 Championship Game, with Oklahoma winning the conference.

Following the season, Oklahoma quarterback Baker Mayfield was the first player from the Big 12 Conference to be selected in the 2018 NFL Draft. He was drafted first overall by the Cleveland Browns. Throughout the season, the Big 12 Conference recognized players of the week. Several Big 12 players were also named to the 2017 College Football All-America Team.

The Colored Hockey League of the Maritimes (CHL) was an all-black ice hockey league founded in Nova Scotia in 1895, which featured teams from across Canada's Maritime Provinces. The league operated for several decades lasting until 1930.

A President's Scholar is a recipient of the academic scholarship awarded by the Government of Singapore annually, to pursue undergraduate education at a university, usually abroad. The scholarship is considered to be the most prestigious public undergraduate scholarship in Singapore awarded to students of Singaporean nationality.

All recipients have legal obligation to serve a bond—in the form of a public service career for a certain period of time, usually ranging from 4 to 6 years, after completing his or her studies in the university.

Shortlisted candidates are interviewed by a selection committee chaired by the Chairman of the Public Service Commission (PSC) of Singapore.

The President's Scholarship by itself does not award any money or lead to any particular career in public service. As such, it is generally paired with another scholarship, which could be either of the following:

Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American educator, civil engineer, and religious leader in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church). He was a member of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles from 1918 until he was excommunicated in 1943 for unlawful cohabitation.

Lyman came from a prominent Mormon family; his father and grandfather had been apostles. He graduated from Brigham Young Academy and earned advanced degrees from the University of Michigan, the University of Chicago, and Cornell University. He married Amy Brown in 1896, who later served as the Relief Society General President. In 1925, Lyman entered into a relationship with Anna Jacobsen Hegsted, which he considered a plural marriage. This led to his excommunication. He was rebaptized into the LDS Church in 1954 but was not reinstated as an apostle.

Rifle bedding is a process used in firearms to increase accuracy and prolong the life of the stock. It involves using epoxy-based materials to create a flush surface between the rifle's action and stock, preventing flexing and movement. Methods include glass bedding, pillar bedding, and using bedding blocks or chassis. Skim bedding is an adjustment to existing glass bedding. Different methods are used depending on the stock type and desired results. Improper bedding can damage the rifle by mechanically locking the action to the stock or ruining the trigger assembly.

True Liberty is an American Christian hardcore band from Richmond, Virginia, formed in 2005. The band's members are Mike Liberty (lead vocalist, lead guitar), Tom Liberty (bass), and Aaron Liberty (drums, background vocals).

True Liberty has released four studio albums: *This Is War* (2007) on Raging Storm Records, *Brotherhood* (2008) independently, *City of the One* (2010) on Wounded Records, and *Marked for Life* (2012) on Thumper Punk Records. They also released a live album, *Give Me True Liberty or Give Me Death* (2014) on Thumper Punk Records. The band's musical genres include Christian hardcore, hardcore punk, and punk rock.

Sir Robin Buchanan Nicholson, FRS FREng (born 12 August 1934) is a British industrial metallurgist and academic, who served as Chief Scientific Adviser, Cabinet Office, from 1983 to 1985. He then joined the board of Rolls-Royce plc, where he served until 2005. He was also a non-executive board member of BP plc and Pilkington plc.

William Howard Ashton (born 19 August 1943), known professionally as Billy J. Kramer, is an English pop singer. With The Dakotas, Kramer was managed by Brian Epstein during the 1960s and scored hits with several Lennon–McCartney compositions never recorded by the Beatles, among them the UK number one "Bad to Me" (1963). Kramer and the Dakotas had a further UK chart-topper in 1964 with "Little Children" and achieved U.S. success as part of the British Invasion. Since the end of the beat boom, Kramer has continued to record and perform. His autobiography, Do You Want to Know a Secret, was published in 2016.

Brooke High School is a public high school in Wellsburg, West Virginia, United States. It is the only high school in the Brooke County School District. Athletic teams compete as the Brooke Bruins in the WVSSAC Class AAA as well as the Ohio Valley Athletic Conference.

The Deceased Correa (in Spanish La Difunta Correa) is a semi-pagan legendary figure in folk-religion, for which a number of people in Argentina and Chile, especially among the popular classes, feel a great devotion. It has spread, in a limited way, to neighbouring countries such as Uruguay. Every year since its inception in 1840, miracles are said to have occurred at the shrine of La Difunta Correa, and thousands of people have visited there to pay their respects. The shrine is situated in the small town of Vallecito, 1160 km from Buenos Aires and 63 km from the city of San Juan.

Stuyvesant Street is one of the oldest streets in the New York City borough of Manhattan. It runs diagonally from 9th Street at Third Avenue to 10th Street near Second Avenue, all within the East Village, Manhattan, neighborhood. The majority of the street is included in the St. Mark's Historic District.

Although the street runs diagonally in relation to the Manhattan street grid, geographically it is one of the few true east–west streets in Manhattan, since most of the grid runs southeast–northwest at a 28.9 degree offset. It is a one-way street, running eastbound.
*Uptown New York* is a 1932 American pre-Code drama film directed by Victor Schertzinger, starring Jack Oakie, Shirley Grey, and Leon Ames. The plot concerns a love triangle and its consequences.

The Gao Empire was a kingdom centered in Gao, located on the Niger River, that existed from approximately the 9th century to 1430. Founded in the 7th century, Gao quickly became a hub for fishing and trade, prospering due to its commercial importance. It was also a major manufacturing center, known for crafting carnelian into beads.

Historical information about the Gao Empire is derived primarily from Arabic geographers and 17th-century chronicles like the Tarikh al-Sudan and Tarikh al-Fattash, supplemented by archaeological evidence, including tombstones discovered at Gao-Saney. By the 10th century, Gao had become a Muslim settlement consisting of two towns. The Islamization of its rulers occurred around the late 11th century, possibly influenced by the Almoravids.

The people of Gao spoke Songhay, a language of the Nile-Saharan family. Towards the end of the 13th century, the Gao Empire was absorbed into the Mali Empire. In the 15th century, the Sunni dynasty rebelled against Mali, eventually leading to the establishment of the Songhai Empire, which succeeded the Gao Empire.
Manilal Gandhi (1892–1956) was the second son of Mohandas Gandhi, who followed in his father's footsteps as an activist.

Terry Mutchler is an American attorney, transparency advocate, and former journalist. She began her career as a reporter for the Associated Press, later becoming the first woman to be appointed as the AP's Statehouse Correspondent for the region in 1993. In 2008, Mutchler was appointed as the first director of the Pennsylvania Office of Open Records. She has since worked in private practice, representing public figures in legal matters. Mutchler is the recipient of the Bob Edgar Public Service Achievement Award and was inducted into the National Freedom of Information Coalition's State Open Government Hall of Fame. She is also the author of a memoir, *Under This Beautiful Dome*.

Vibal Publishing House, Inc. (also known as Vibal Group, Inc., Vibal, or VPHI) is a major publishing house in the Philippines based in Quezon City. Vibal was founded in 1953 by Hilarion P. Vibal and by his wife, Esther Asuncion-Vibal. Vibal is a textbook, reference materials, and multimedia products publisher and is the biggest in the country. In 2011, in partnership with Procter & Gamble, Vibal introduces digital classrooms and low-cost digital education tablets, for the digitization of education in the country.

Kenny Francis (born December 1, 1969) is an American NASCAR crew chief. He began racing go-karts at age eight, winning a state championship. Francis began working as a crew chief in the NASCAR Cup Series in 2003, winning 17 races and 21 poles. He won the 1999 NASCAR Winston Cup Series championship working for Robert Yates Racing.
Felix Johannes Oinas (1893-2004) was an Estonian folklorist and linguist who emigrated to the United States after World War II.
Abderahmane Hachoud (Lang-ar; born July 2, 1988, in El Attaf) is an Algerian professional football player.
Christian Albert, Burgrave and Count of Dohna (also known as Christoph Albert, 10 December 1621 in Küstrin – 14 December 1677 in Gartz) was a German nobleman from the House of Dohna.

Louis-Henri Murger, also known as Henri Murger and Henry Murger (27 March 1822 – 28 January 1861), was a French novelist and poet.

He is chiefly distinguished as the author of the 1851 book Scènes de la vie de bohème (Scenes of Bohemian Life), which is based on his own experiences as a desperately poor writer living in a Parisian garret (the top floor of buildings, where artists often lived) and as a member of a loose club of friends who called themselves "the water drinkers" (because they were too poor to afford wine). In his writing he combines instinct with pathos, humour, and sadness. The book is the basis for the 1896 opera La bohème by Puccini, Leoncavallo's opera of the same name, and, at greater removes, the zarzuela Bohemios (Amadeu Vives), the 1930 operetta Das Veilchen vom Montmartre (Kálmán), and the 1996 Broadway musical Rent. He wrote lyrics as well as novels and stories, the chief being La Chanson de Musette, "a tear," says Gautier, "which has become a pearl of poetry".

Henry Nicholas John Gunther (June 6, 1895 – November 11, 1918) was an American soldier and possibly the last soldier of any of the belligerents to be killed during World War I. He was killed at 10:59 a.m., about one minute before the Armistice was to take effect at 11:00 a.m.

Gunther had recently been demoted, and was seeking to regain his rank just before the war ended.

The Capture of Santiago was an English victory in November 1585 during the Anglo-Spanish War. An English expedition led by Francis Drake and Christopher Carleill attacked the Spanish-controlled Cape Verde Islands. After landing on November 11, Drake's forces captured Cidade Velha, looting the town and capturing ships. They then burned São Domingos and razed Praia. On November 28, Drake, having failed to secure a ransom, ordered Cidade Velha burned before sailing to the Caribbean. The Spanish later strengthened the island's defenses.
Boulos Fahmy (born 1956) is the Chief Justice of the Supreme Constitutional Court of Egypt, assuming office on 9 February 2022.

The first USS Arizona was an iron-hulled, side-wheel merchant steamship. Seized by the Confederate States of America in 1862 during the American Civil War, she was captured later the same year by the United States Navy.

St Richard's Church is an Anglican church in the town of Haywards Heath in the district of Mid Sussex, one of seven local government districts in the English county of West Sussex. The present reinforced concrete and brick structure replaced a temporary building which was a daughter church to Haywards Heath's parish church, St Wilfrid's; the new church soon became parished in its own right to reflect the rapid population growth in the northern part of the town. English Heritage has listed the 1930s building at Grade II for its architectural and historical importance.
The Weatherly Model 201 is a 1960s American agricultural monoplane designed and built by the Weatherly Aviation Company of Hollister, California.

Nick Rotteveel (IPA-nl; born January 6, 1989), professionally known as Nicky Romero or Monocule, is a Dutch DJ, record producer and remixer from Amerongen, Utrecht Province. He has worked with, and received support from DJs, such as Tiësto, Fedde le Grand, Sander van Doorn, David Guetta, Calvin Harris, Armand van Helden, Avicii and Hardwell. He currently ranks at number 26 on DJ Mag's annual Top 100 DJs poll. He is known for his viral hit song "Toulouse".
The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military aircraft developed from an American civilian design and used primarily for artillery spotting during World War II.

Riffs is a live album by American saxophonist Jimmy Lyons. It was recorded on September 13–14, 1980 at Le Dreher, a jazz club in Paris, and was released in 1982 on the hat MUSICS label. The album features Lyons on alto saxophone, Karen Borca on bassoon, Jay Oliver on bass, and Paul Murphy on drums.

Brooke High School is a public, coeducational high school in Wellsburg, West Virginia, established in 1969. The school's athletic teams are known as the Bruins, and they have won state championships in football and boys' basketball. The cheerleading squads have won multiple state titles.

Opendatasoft is a French company that offers data sharing software. Based in Paris, it also has a subsidiary in Boston (United States) and an office in Nantes (France).

Opendatasoft has developed a tool for sharing and reusing the data of companies and public administrations. Its software lets you organize, share, and visualize any type of data. The software can be used by both private companies and public entities

Interstate 285 (I-285) is a 23.49-mile-long (37.80 km) auxiliary Interstate Highway in North Carolina, serving as a partial bypass of Lexington. It is concurrent with US 52, US 29, US 70 and I-85 Bus for a portion of its route. Its southern terminus is at I-85 in Lexington, and its northern terminus is at I-40 in Winston-Salem, where the freeway continues as US 52/NC 8.

Approved in 2005, the route was signed as "Future Interstate 285 Corridor" until improvements were completed in late 2017. The I-285 designation was officially approved in February 2018, and route shields were installed in November 2018. There are plans to extend I-285 north to a future I-74/I-274 interchange and potentially widen the route to six lanes.

Yawalapiti (Jaulapiti) is an Arawakan language of Brazil. The Agavotaguerra (Agavotoqueng) reportedly spoke the same language. Speakers of the language live in a village along the edge of the river Tuatuari, a tributary of the Kuluene River, located in the southern part of the Xingu Indigenous Park (Upper Xingu), in the state of Mato Grosso.

The 2014 FedEx Cup Playoffs were a series of four golf tournaments that took place from August 21 to September 14, 2014. The tournaments were played on the PGA Tour. Billy Horschel ultimately won the FedEx Cup.

Ryan Higa (born June 6, 1990), also known as nigahiga, is an American YouTuber and actor. He began posting videos on YouTube in 2006 with Sean Fujiyoshi while in high school, initially lip-syncing to songs before expanding to comedic skits. Higa is of Okinawan descent and holds a black belt in judo. He briefly studied nuclear medicine at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas before dedicating himself to creating online videos.

Higa's YouTube channel, nigahiga, was the first to reach 3 million subscribers in 2010. He later created a second channel, HigaTV, for vlogs and behind-the-scenes content. In 2008, Higa and Fujiyoshi starred in their first feature-length film, *Ryan and Sean's Not So Excellent Adventure*. Higa also appeared in *Supah Ninjas*, *Tell Me How I Die*, and *Finding 'Ohana*. In 2016, Higa co-founded the parody K-pop group Boys Generally Asian (BgA), whose songs topped the iTunes K-pop charts.

Beyond YouTube, Higa co-founded Ninja Melk, LLC, launching an energy drink in 2019. He also published his memoir, *Ryan Higa's How to Write Good*, in 2017. Higa endorsed Andrew Yang for president in 2020 and has publicly discussed his agnostic beliefs and ADHD diagnosis.
St. Dominic's Abbey is a medieval Dominican abbey and National Monument located in Cashel, Ireland.

Rosa Amelia Guzmán was a Salvadoran journalist, feminist and suffragette. Her 1950 speech to the Constituent Assembly was instrumental in women gaining, not just the right to vote, but the rights of citizenship on 14 September. She was one of the first four women elected to serve in the Legislative Assembly of El Salvador.
Taiwanese legislative election in 1992: KMT won 95 of 161 seats.

Paleontology in New Hampshire is limited by the state's geological history. The state has no fossil record dating to the early Paleozoic due to the prevalence of igneous and heavily metamorphosed rocks from that period. However, evidence from neighboring states suggests that New Hampshire was likely covered by a warm, shallow sea during the Silurian period, home to creatures like brachiopods, bryozoans, corals, crinoids, and trilobites. Fossils from this era have been found in the Fitch Formation near Littleton and Lisbon.

During the Devonian period, geological uplift led to mountain building, and the state was home to branchiopods, crinoids, and gastropods. Fossil sites from this time include locations near Littleton and Whitefield. Uplift continued through the Carboniferous and Permian periods, causing erosion and a lack of rock record for fossil preservation. Similarly, erosion during the Mesozoic era resulted in a scant rock record, with no dinosaur fossils ever discovered in the state.

The Cenozoic era saw further erosion, with no rock record for the Tertiary period. During the Quaternary, glaciers covered much of New Hampshire, and the plant life resembled modern arctic Canada. Megafauna like woolly mammoths and mastodons roamed the area during interglacial periods. Discoveries of mastodon fossils off the Isles of Shoals in 2013 and a mammoth tooth in 2014 highlight this period.

The 1912–13 season was Blackpool F.C.'s 13th season in the Football League. They finished bottom of Division Two. George Wilson was the club's top scorer with ten goals. In the FA Cup, Blackpool were knocked out in the first round after a replay against Tottenham Hotspur.
Laura Rajnović Evetović (Serbian Cyrillic: Лаура Рајновић Еветовић; born 1 June 1975) is a politician and administrator in Serbia. She served in the Assembly of Vojvodina from 2012 to 2020.

Unconscious (or intuitive) communication is the subtle, unintentional, unconscious cues that provide information to another individual. It can be verbal (speech patterns, physical activity while speaking, or the tone of voice of an individual) or it can be nonverbal (facial expressions and body language). Some psychologists instead use the term honest signals because such cues are involuntary behaviors that often convey emotion whereas body language can be controlled. Many decisions are based on unconscious communication, which is interpreted and created in the right hemisphere of the brain. The right hemisphere is dominant in perceiving and expressing body language, facial expressions, verbal cues, and other indications that have to do with emotion but it does not exclusively deal with the unconscious.

Little is known about the unconscious mind or about how decisions are made based on unconscious communications except that they are always unintentional. There are two types of unconscious communications: intrapersonal and interpersonal.

Research has shown that human conscious attention can attend to 5–9 items simultaneously. All other information is processed by the unconscious mind. For example, the unconscious mind sometimes picks up on and relates nonverbal cues about an individual based on how they have arranged their settings such as their home or place of work.

Angela Dufresne (born 1969) is an American painter known for her contemporary work. She received a BFA from the Kansas City Art Institute and an MFA from the Tyler School of Art at Temple University. Dufresne's paintings reference art history, film, personal experiences, and often depict mythical creatures and eroticism. She teaches at the Rhode Island School of Design.

In mathematics, the dimension of a vector space V is the cardinality (i.e., the number of vectors) of a basis of V over its base field. It is sometimes called Hamel dimension (after Georg Hamel) or algebraic dimension to distinguish it from other types of dimension.

For every vector space there exists a basis, and all bases of a vector space have equal cardinality; as a result, the dimension of a vector space is uniquely defined. We say is finite-dimensional if the dimension of is finite, and infinite-dimensional if its dimension is infinite.

The dimension of the vector space over the field can be written as or as read "dimension of over". When can be inferred from context, is typically written.
The Oberkommando der Luftwaffe (lit. 'Upper Command of the Air Force'; abbreviated OKL) was the high command of the air force (Luftwaffe) of Nazi Germany.

The Aleutian tern (Onychoprion aleuticus) is a migratory bird living in the subarctic region of the globe most of the year. It is frequently associated with the Arctic tern, which it closely resembles. While both species have a black cap, the Aleutian tern may be distinguished by its white forehead (although juvenile Arctic terns also have white foreheads). During breeding season, the Arctic terns have bright red bills, feet, and legs while those of the Aleutian terns are black.

Onychoprion aleuticus has not been thoroughly studied yet. If its winter migratory range was completely unknown until the late 1980s; it is now known that many Aleutian terns spend the winter near the Equator in the western Pacific. The Aleutian tern breeds in wide-ranging coastal colonies only in Alaska and eastern Siberia. Although Alaskan and Siberian populations are not well monitored, both are thought to be in significant decline.

MyPertamina is a loyalty program by PT Pertamina, an Indonesian state-owned oil and gas company. It began in December 2016 as merchandise outlets, and in August 2017, expanded to include a mobile app and electronic card.

Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP) is a public, not-for-profit research unit in the Department of Community Health Sciences within the Rady Faculty of Health Sciences at the University of Manitoba (Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada). MCHP provides and uses population-based administrative data to inform health policy, social policy and health and social service delivery provincially, nationally and internationally. MCHP also creates population-based research on health and the social determinants of health.

Helene Hewitt OBE is a British climate scientist who is a research fellow at the Met Office. Her research considers climate and ocean models. Hewitt serves on the CLIVAR Ocean Model Development Panel. She was awarded an Order of the British Empire in the 2022 Birthday Honours.

The 2014 Harris County shooting was a mass shooting that occurred on July 9, 2014, near Spring, Texas. Ronald Lee Haskell, motivated by a domestic dispute, killed six members of the Stay family, including four children. One 15-year-old survived and identified Haskell, who was later apprehended after a police chase and standoff. He was convicted of capital murder and sentenced to death.

Pivotal (19 January 1993 – 19 November 2021) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and sire. In a racing career restricted to six races between October 1995 and August 1996 he established himself as one of the leading sprinters in Europe. His most important wins came in the King's Stand Stakes and the Nunthorpe Stakes as a three-year-old in the summer of 1996. He was then retired to stud where he became an exceptionally successful breeding stallion.

The 2010 South Carolina gubernatorial election was held on November 2, 2010. Republican Nikki Haley was elected governor, succeeding Republican Mark Sanford. During the election, Vincent Sheheen received an endorsement from the South Carolina Chamber of Commerce, while Nikki Haley received endorsements from the National Rifle Association and South Citizens for Life. Several debates were held, including one sponsored by the South Carolina Republican Party and moderated by MSNBC hosts Joe Scarborough and Mika Brzezinski.

Buttlejorrk is a parish in Victoria, Australia, located at 37°34′44′′S 144°40′41′′E. Historically, it encompassed the settlement of Aitken's Gap, a key staging post during the gold rush era. Aitken's Gap featured hotels, stores, and a police station, serving travelers heading to the goldfields. The Gap Inn and Bald Hill hotels were prominent establishments, with archaeological evidence remaining today.

The opening of the Bendigo railway in 1859 led to the decline of Aitken's Gap. A Church of England school operated from 1857 until 1900. Today, a row of Monterey Pines along the Calder Highway marks the former township. A fire in 1944 destroyed numerous houses in the area. The Aitkens Gap Gaol, built in 1857, was relocated to Sunbury in 1989 and is now part of the Old Sunbury Township heritage drive.
Jamil Baroody (1905 – death date unknown) was a Lebanese diplomat. A representative of Saudi Arabia to the United Nations from its founding, he was known for his oratory.

Robert Neelly Bellah (February 23, 1927 – July 30, 2013) was an American sociologist and the Elliott Professor of Sociology at the University of California, Berkeley. He was internationally known for his work related to the sociology of religion.

A mobile is a type of kinetic sculpture constructed of objects that are suspended and balanced in such a way that they move in response to air currents or direct contact. The term "mobile" was first suggested by Marcel Duchamp in 1931 to describe the early mechanized sculptures of Alexander Calder, and was initially synonymous with "kinetic art". While early mobiles were motor-driven, the term later came to refer more specifically to Calder's free-moving creations.

Calder's mobiles typically consist of brightly colored, abstract shapes fashioned from sheet metal, connected by wires in a balanced arrangement. The sequential attachment of additional objects results in a final creation with many balanced parts capable of independent or collective movement. While Calder's work is most closely associated with the term "mobile", other artists such as Man Ray, Armando Reverón and Bruno Munari experimented with similar concepts.

The Manitoba Centre for Health Policy (MCHP) is an academic institute founded in 1991 at the University of Manitoba. It maintains a large population-based data repository with health, social, and economic data on Manitobans. MCHP's research focuses on population health, social determinants of health, and the well-being of specific populations. The center collaborates with government and other stakeholders.

St Richard's Church is a Church of England parish church in Haywards Heath, West Sussex, UK. Originally a chapel of ease called the Chapel of the Holy Spirit, it was renamed St Richard's Church in 1916. The current church building was designed in 1937 by L. Keir Hett in an Art Deco/Scandinavian style and is primarily constructed of reinforced concrete. Consecrated in 1938 and completed in 1942, it replaced a temporary metal church. The church was designated a Grade II listed building in 2000.

The National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), established in 1975, is a "nonpartisan public officials’ association composed of sitting state legislators" from the states, territories and commonwealths of the United States.

The 1999 Brickyard 400 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series race held at Indianapolis Motor Speedway. The race was won by Dale Jarrett, who led 117 of the 160 laps, becoming the second two-time winner of the event. Jeff Gordon won the pole position. The race was broadcast on ABC with a Nielsen rating of 4.6/15.

The 2017 Big 12 Conference football season was the 22nd season of Big 12 Conference football, taking place during the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. The season began with non-conference play on Saturday, September 2, 2017, when all 10 of the league's teams played their first non-conference opponents. Big 12 Conference play began on Saturday, September 23, 2017.

The 2017 season was the sixth for the Big 12 since the 2010–13 Big 12 Conference realignment brought the Big 12 membership to its current form. The conference had ten members: Baylor, Iowa State, Kansas, Kansas State, Oklahoma, Oklahoma State, TCU, Texas, Texas Tech and West Virginia. The Big 12 is a Power Five conference under the College Football Playoff format, along with the ACC, the Big Ten, the Pac-12 and the SEC.

As a ten-team league, the Big 12 will played a nine-game, round-robin conference schedule. Each member will played three non-conference games–one of was required to be against another Power Five conference foe. The regular season was followed by a conference championship game played between the regular season champion and regular season runner-up. The 2017 Big 12 Championship Game was held Saturday, December 2, 2017, at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.

The Slinkard Fire was a wildfire in Mono County in California in the United States. The fire was reported on August 29, 2017. It was caused by a lightning strike. The fire was fully contained on September 12, after it had burned 8,925 acres (36 km2).

Michael Kevin O'Loughlin (born 20 February 1977) is a former Australian rules footballer who played for the Sydney Swans in the Australian Football League (AFL). Nicknamed "Micky O" and "Magic Mick", he was an All-Australian twice (1997, 2000), won Sydney's best and fairest award in 1998, and played in the Swans' 2005 premiership team. O'Loughlin was also the club's leading goalkicker twice (2000–01). In 2009, he was awarded the Madden Medal. After retiring, he coached the Indigenous All-Stars and co-founded the GO Foundation with Adam Goodes, focusing on education for Indigenous Australians.

The National Hockey Stadium was a stadium located in Milton Keynes, England. Built in 1995, it served as the home of England Hockey until 2003. In 1997, it hosted the FIH Men's Junior World Cup, and in 2000, the FIH Women's Qualifying Tournament.

In 2003, Wimbledon F.C. leased the stadium, converting it to a grass pitch and adding temporary stands. The club, later renamed Milton Keynes Dons, played there until 2007, when they moved to Stadium MK. After their departure, the stadium was deemed no longer viable as a hockey venue, especially with the planned Olympic Hockey Centre for the 2012 Olympics. Demolition began in December 2009 and was completed by March 2010.

Peter Blundell Jones (4 January 1949 – 19 August 2016) was a British architect and architectural historian. He trained as an architect at the Architectural Association School, and held academic positions at the University of Cambridge and London South Bank University. He was a professor of architecture at the University of Sheffield from 1994 until his death in 2016.

He was a prolific author on architectural history and theory. As well as being a regular contributor to the Architectural Review, he wrote or collaborated on monographs of the work of Erik Gunnar Asplund, Hans Scharoun, Erich Mendelsohn, Hugo Häring and Günter Behnisch.

Jones died on 19 August 2016 at the age of 67.
The Rafz train crash occurred at approximately 6.43 am on 20 February 2015. An S-Bahn and an Interregio express train collided at Rafz railway station in Rafz, Canton of Zürich, Switzerland.
London Bourne (1793 – unknown) was a Barbadian businessman, landowner, and civil rights activist.

Adil Isgandarov (1910–1978) was an Azerbaijani actor, theater and film director, and pedagogue. He directed at the Azerbaijan State Academic National Drama Theatre and Azerbaijanfilm. Isgandarov was also a deputy in the Supreme Soviet.

Grange Barn, located in Coggeshall, United Kingdom, is a Grade I listed building and one of the oldest surviving timber-framed buildings in Europe, dating back to the 13th century. Originally associated with Coggeshall Abbey, the barn remained in agricultural use until 1960, after which it fell into disrepair. In 1982, Braintree District Council acquired the barn and initiated its restoration, which was completed in 1985 and received a Europa Nostra award. In 1989, the barn was entrusted to the National Trust for preservation. Today, Grange Barn is open to the public, featuring exhibits on its history and local woodworking tools. It is also available for special events.

The Gao Empire preceded the Songhai Empire in the region of the Middle Niger. It owes its name to the town of Gao located at the eastern Niger bend. In the ninth century CE, it was considered to be the most powerful West African kingdom.

Gao was founded in the 7th century. Because of its placement along the Niger River, it was a profitable location for fisherman to settle. Gao is one of the oldest trading centers in western Africa. Because of the placement of Gao, Gao became the capital for the Songhai (or Songhay) Empire in the early 11th century. Gao was able to flourish as the capital of the Songhai Empire, most notably via the trans-Saharan trade of gold, copper, slaves, and salt. Gao was annexed by the rulers of the kingdom of Mali in 1325, but Songhai would eventually retain control of it 40 years later.

There are no surviving indigenous written records that date from before the middle of the 17th century. Our knowledge of the early history of the town relies on the writings of external Arabic geographers living in Morocco, Egypt and Andalusia, who never visited the region.

Sir Frederick Mann KCMG (2 May 1869 – 29 May 1958) was an Australian judge who served as Chief Justice of Victoria from 1935 to 1944. He was also the Lieutenant Governor of Victoria from 1936 to 1945.

Born in South Australia, Mann studied law at the University of Melbourne while working for the Victorian Government. He served in the Boer War and later specialized as a barrister in common law and equity. He was appointed to the Supreme Court of Victoria in 1919 and was knighted in 1933.
Nathan Sawaya (born July 10, 1973) is an American artist who creates sculptures using LEGO bricks. He is recognized by The LEGO Group as a LEGO Certified Professional.

Buttlejorrk is a parish of the County of Bourke located to the west of Sunbury, in Victoria, Australia and a neighbourhood within the locality of Diggers Rest. It was named in 1839 by the surveyor William Darke. A township developed and was initially known as Aitken's Gap. By the 1890s it had commonly become known as Buttlejorrk. A Post Office opened on 5 September 1856 as The Gap, was renamed Buttlejork (sic) in 1875 and closed in 1919. One of the early settlers in the area was George Millett. George and his wife Suzanne established the Bald Hill Hotel, the first staging stop out of Melbourne on the road to the goldfields at Bendigo. The hotel was licensed in 1853 until 1907. The Aitken's Gap Gaol, now located in the forecourt of the Sunbury Police Station stood beside the hotel

Marie Fillunger (1850–1930) was an Austrian classical singer and voice teacher. She studied at the Vienna Conservatory and the Hochschule in Berlin, and taught at the Royal Manchester College of Music.

The American Federation of Aviculture, Inc. (AFA) is a national organization dedicated to aviculture, whose purpose is to educate the public and assist members regarding best practices for keeping and living with exotic birds. Local affiliate bird clubs throughout the United States, including Puerto Rico, along with national and international specialty organizations; comprise the Federation. The American Federation of Aviculture is registered as a non-profit 501(c)3 educational organization, with a business office located in Austin, Texas.

AFA members work to promote responsible breeding and care of birds. The volunteer organization provides assistance and resources to all people involved with companion birds including breeders, owners, researchers, vets and rescue organizations. AFA members actively support research projects designed to make life better for parrots in their native habitats.

Solder paste is a material used in electronics manufacturing to connect components to printed circuit boards (PCBs). It consists of powdered solder suspended in flux, which holds components in place until the soldering process. Solder paste is typically applied using a stencil-printing process. The size and shape of the metal particles in the solder paste determines how well the paste will "print". Solder pastes are classified into three types based on the flux types: Rosin based fluxes, Water-soluble fluxes and No-clean flux. Solder paste must be refrigerated when transported and stored in an airtight container.

Luise Kähler (12 January 1869 – 22 September 1955) was a German socialist, trade union leader and politician. She was one of a small number of women union officials that held a prominent position within Germany's trade unions in the first half of the twentieth century. She was a member of the Prussian Landtag from 1923 to 1933. She opposed National Socialism and was largely inactive after Hitler came to power in 1933. After World War II, she became a founding member of the Socialist Unity Party of Germany, the ruling party in the German Democratic Republic.

Arnot Art Museum, opened 1913, is a municipal art museum located at 235 Lake Street in Elmira, New York. Its permanent collection includes 17th-, 18th-, and 19th-century European paintings; and 19th- and 20th-century American art. Its 21st-century collection focuses on contemporary representational art. The building is a contributing property in the Elmira Civic Historic District.
*Callulops fojaensis* is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae. It is only known from the Foja Mountains in Indonesia. Its conservation status is Data Deficient.

The Committee of the Verkhovna Rada on issues of ecological policy (Ukrainian: Комітет Верховної Ради України з питань екологічної політики) is a standing committee in the Verkhovna Rada (Ukraine's parliament). It was formed on June 28, 1990, as the "Permanent Commission of the Verkhovna Rada of the Ukrainian SSR on issues of ecology and sustainable use of natural resources". The committee's jurisdiction is within Ukraine.

The 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League knockout phase began on 9 December 2020 with the round of 32 and ended with the final on 16 May 2021 at the Gamla Ullevi in Gothenburg, Sweden, to decide the champions of the 2020–21 UEFA Women's Champions League. A total of 32 teams competed in the knockout phase.

Ernest Carlos Camacho (born February 1, 1955) is a former professional baseball player who pitched in the major leagues from 1980 to 1981 and 1983 to 1990. His best season came with the 1984 Cleveland Indians, when he led the team with 23 saves.

MyPertamina is a digital financial service platform from Pertamina that integrated with the apps LinkAja. This application is used for non-cash fuel oil payments at Pertamina's public fueling stations.

Abd al-Malik ibn Umar ibn Marwan (c. 718 – c. 778) was an Umayyad prince and military leader. He was a grandson of Caliph Marwan I and initially based in Egypt. Following the Abbasid Revolution in 750, he fled to al-Andalus (Islamic Spain) around 754/755. There, he became a leading general for Abd al-Rahman I, the founder of the Emirate of Córdoba. Abd al-Malik played a key role in consolidating Umayyad control over al-Andalus, notably suppressing a major revolt in 774. His family remained prominent in the Emirate, with several descendants serving as viziers and governors.

Susan Lynn Gordon (July 27, 1949 – December 11, 2011) was an American child actress who appeared in films and numerous episodes of television programs such as The Twilight Zone, My Three Sons, and The Donna Reed Show.

Peter Blundell Jones (birth date unknown) was an architectural journalist, professor, and architect. He is known for his writing on architecture, including a book on Hans Scharoun.

After graduating from the Architectural Association School of Architecture (AA) in 1972, he wrote his book on Scharoun. He then designed The Round House at Stoke Canon, Devon, for his parents. He taught at the North London Polytechnic and the University of Bath, while also writing for architectural publications. He was an assistant lecturer at Cambridge University (1979-83). In 1992, he was named architectural journalist of the year, and in 1994, he was appointed professor at the University of Sheffield School of Architecture.
Mali–Netherlands relations involve diplomatic ties through embassies, Dutch contributions to the UN mission in Northern Mali, and related political consequences.

According to Theravada Buddhism's Pali canon's Buddhavamsa and its commentary, Phussa is the twenty-first of twenty-seven Buddhas who preceded the historical Gotama Buddha. He was also the second Buddha of the Maṇḍa kalpa.

In the Buddhavamsa, he is described as:

Phussa Buddha removed the darkness, calmed the world and gave the Dhamma rain.

Phussa was 58 cubits, or 87 feet tall and was frequently compared with the sun and the moon.

A zograscope is an optical device for enhancing the sense of depth perception in a two-dimensional image. It typically consists of a large magnifying lens and a mirror or angled viewing surface, allowing the viewer to look at a print or drawing in such a way that its perspective is enhanced, creating a more realistic and immersive experience. Some have described the experience as a form of "virtual reality".

The device works by magnifying the image and minimizing cues that indicate its flatness. The edges of the image are blocked by the frame of the lens, and the light coming from the lens to the eye is collimated, preventing accommodation. Early prints sometimes used color contrast to enhance the perception of depth, with saturated colors on some objects and pale washes in the background.

Zograscopes may also enhance depth perception by creating binocular stereopsis, where each eye views the image from a slightly different position. This can result in binocular disparity, which creates an overall slant of the picture surface. Even if the binocular disparity is incorrect, the stereoscopic information tends to integrate with the other depth information in the image.

A simple zograscope can be constructed using a frame and a large, magnifying Fresnel lens placed over a computer monitor displaying a photograph of a natural scene.
Laura Rajnović Evetović is a Serbian economist and politician. She was elected to the Assembly of Vojvodina in the 2012 and 2016 provincial elections.

Winter Haven Hospital is a 527-bed non-profit hospital in Winter Haven, Florida, founded in 1926. It is part of the BayCare Health System since 2013. The hospital has expanded over the years, adding specialty centers such as the Cassidy Cancer Center and the Bostick Heart Center. It also operates facilities outside its main campus, including the Regency Center for Women and Infants and several Family Health Centers. Winter Haven Hospital is the fourth-largest private sector employer in Polk County.
Hawthorn railway station is located on the Lilydale, Belgrave and Alamein lines in Victoria, Australia. It serves the eastern Melbourne suburb of Hawthorn, and it opened on 13 April 1861.
Jeremy Maurice Jacobs (born January 21, 1940) is the chairman of Delaware North, owner of the Boston Bruins, and chairman of the NHL Board of Governors.
Eric Sharp, Baron Sharp of Grimsdyke, CBE (17 August 1916 – 2 May 1994) was a British businessman and Member of the House of Lords.

Oreophrynella quelchii, commonly known as the Roraima black frog or Roraima bush toad, is a species of toad in the family Bufonidae. This species is restricted to the transboundary summit of Mount Roraima in Venezuela, Guyana and Brazil, and to the nearby Wei-Assipu-tepui on the Brazil–Guyana border. It has been recorded at elevations of 2,300–2,800 m (7,500–9,200 ft) above sea level.

Sylvia Fine Kaye (August 29, 1913 – October 28, 1991) was an American lyricist, composer, and producer. Many of her compositions and productions were performed by her husband, comedian Danny Kaye. Fine was a Peabody Award-winner and was nominated for two Academy Awards and two Emmys during her career. She won an Emmy award in 1976 for children's special.

Aloysius Edward "Pie" Piechota (January 19, 1914 – June 13, 1996) was a professional baseball player whose career spanned 15 seasons, two of which were spent with the Major League Baseball (MLB) Boston Bees / Braves from 1940 to 1941. Piechota, a pitcher, compiled an earned run average (ERA) of 5.66, allowing 39 earned runs off of 68 hits, 6 home runs, and 42 walks while recording 18 strikeouts over 62 innings pitched. Piechota also played in 14 seasons of minor league baseball. He made his MLB debut at the age of 26 and was officially listed as standing 6 feet 0 inches (183 cm) and weighing 195 pounds (88 kg).

"Let It Go" is a song by Hong Kong singer and actor Jacky Cheung (Cantonese pinyin: Hok Yau)(中文名:张学友)written by Roxanne Seeman, Daniel Lindstrom and Daniele Musto for Cheung's album Private Corner (2010). Cantonese lyrics were written by Kenny So (乔星 Qiao Xing). The song was released to radio by Universal Music in April 2010 as the fourth single and is the seventh track on the album.

Petro Pysarchuk (Lang-uk, born June 6, 1955, in Chemeryntsi, village, Peremyshliany Raion, Lviv Oblast, Ukrainian SSR) is a Ukrainian Politician and Entrepreneur. People's Deputy to the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine 4th, 5th and 6th convocations.
Félix Jules Méline (1838–1925) was a French statesman who served as Prime Minister of France from 1896 to 1898.

Mansfield is a borough located in east-central Tioga County, Pennsylvania, United States, in the Tioga River valley. It is situated at the intersection of U.S. Route 6 and U.S. Business Route 15, about 36 miles (58 km) southwest of Elmira, New York.
Legislative elections were held in Taiwan on 19 December 1992.

Abd al-Malik ibn Umar ibn Marwan ibn al-Hakam (Lang-ar; c. 718– c. 778), also known as al-Marwani, was an Umayyad prince, general and governor of Seville under the first Umayyad emir of al-Andalus (Islamic Spain), Abd al-Rahman I (r. 756–788). He led two major campaigns in 758 and 774, the first against the previous ruler of al-Andalus Yusuf ibn Abd al-Rahman al-Fihri and the second against the rebellious troops of Seville and Beja. His victories solidified the Umayyad emirate's control of western al-Andalus. His descendants continued to play important political and military roles in the Emirate well into the 10th century.

Victory Day over Nazism in World War II (Ukrainian: День перемоги над нацизмом у Другій світовій війні) is a Ukrainian holiday celebrated annually on May 9. Established in 2015, it replaced the Soviet-era Victory Day, following the country's decommunization laws. The holiday aims to shift the focus from glorifying war to honoring personal histories, similar to Remembrance Day. The remembrance poppy is the official symbol.

Charles Hargens (1893-1997) was an American artist and illustrator known for his Western illustrations and attention to detail. He studied at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts and in Paris. Hargens worked on book jackets, magazine illustrations, and advertisements for companies like Stetson and Coca-Cola, with work appearing in *The Saturday Evening Post* and *Boys Life*. In 1940, he moved to Carversville, Pennsylvania. Hargens donated his art studio and many of his Western works to the Dakota Discovery Museum. He was also a scoutmaster for over 30 years.

Mam Tor (also known as the "Shivering Mountain") is a 517 m (1,696 ft) hill near Castleton in the High Peak area of Derbyshire, England. Part of the Peak District, it lies on the southern edge of the Dark Peak overlooking the White Peak. Mam Tor forms a link between Rushup Edge and the Great Ridge, separating the Hope Valley from Edale and making it a popular location for walking.

The hill is composed of Carboniferous rocks, with black shales at the base and sandstone above. A notable feature is the active landslide on its southeast side, which began approximately 4,000 years ago due to the weak shales beneath the sandstone. This landslide caused the closure of the old Mam Tor road in 1979, which had been repeatedly repaired due to the ongoing ground movement. A diversion for light vehicles now uses the nearby Winnats Pass.

The summit of Mam Tor is encircled by a late Bronze Age and early Iron Age hill fort, with evidence of occupation dating back to around 1200 BC. The site includes Bronze Age burial mounds and over a hundred platforms where timber huts once stood. The hill fort and burial mounds are designated as a Scheduled Ancient Monument.
Félix Jules Méline (IPA-fr; 20 May 1838 – 21 December 1925) was a French statesman, Prime Minister of France from 1896 to 1898.

The Arnot Art Museum is located in Elmira, New York. It is housed in the former mansion of banker Matthias Hallenback Arnot, built in 1833, with a picture gallery added in the 1890s. The museum's collection includes European and American art, with works by William-Adolphe Bouguereau, Albert Bierstadt, and others. A new wing was added in 1982.

Woodford Island is an island in New South Wales, Australia, near the Far-North Coast. It is mostly flood plain, supporting sugar cane and milk production. The island has approximately 300 residents, with the largest town being Brushgrove. Once a major trading hub, the island has diminished in size over the past century. Tourist Drive 22 travels across the island.

The 2010 South Carolina gubernatorial election took place on November 2, 2010. Incumbent Republican Governor Mark Sanford was term limited and unable to seek re-election. Primary elections took place on June 8, 2010, and a runoff election, as was necessary on the Republican side, was held two weeks later on June 22.

Republican Nikki Haley defeated Democrat Vincent Sheheen in the general election by a margin of 4.5%. As of 2018, this is the closest that the Democrats have come to winning the governorship of South Carolina since their last victory in 1998. This is the 1st open seat election since 1994.

*Formula One 99* is a racing video game developed by Studio 33 and published by Psygnosis. It is part of the *Formula One* series. The game was released for the PlayStation in Europe in 1999, in Japan on October 21, 1999, and in North America on October 31, 1999. A Microsoft Windows version was released in Europe on December 10, 1999, and in North America in 1999.

The PlayStation version of *Formula One 99* received positive reviews. Reviewers praised its gameplay and sense of speed, with *Official UK PlayStation Magazine* stating that it "put the series back on track". *GameSpot* highlighted the game's great control and extensive settings, while *IGN* called it "the best F1 game on the PlayStation". *Famitsu* gave the PlayStation version a score of 28 out of 40.

The DR Class 52.80 was a class of 200 "Rekolok" (reconstructed locomotives) rebuilt from DRB Class 52 locomotives by the Deutsche Reichsbahn (DR) in East Germany between 1960 and 1967. The rebuild included a new combustion-chambered boiler, welded cylinders, and a new driver's cab. A number of these locomotives survive today, including one extensively rebuilt example owned by DLM AG in Switzerland.

William Howard Ashton (born August 19, 1943), known professionally as Billy J. Kramer, is an English pop singer. As the leader of Billy J. Kramer with the Dakotas, he was managed by Brian Epstein, who also managed The Beatles. Kramer was given several songs written by John Lennon and Paul McCartney, launching him to stardom in the 1960s. His hits included "Do You Want to Know a Secret?", "Bad to Me", and "Little Children". In 2013, he released a new album, *I Won the Fight*, and in 2016, he published his autobiography, *Do You Want to Know A Secret*.

Solder paste is used in the manufacture of printed circuit boards to connect surface mount components to pads on the board. It is also possible to solder through-hole pin in paste components by printing solder paste in and over the holes. The sticky paste temporarily holds components in place; the board is then heated, melting the paste and forming a mechanical bond as well as an electrical connection. The paste is applied to the board by jet printing, stencil printing or syringe; then the components are put in place by a pick-and-place machine or by hand.
Babócsa is a village in Somogy county, Hungary, near Croatia.

The C. H. Moore Homestead, in Clinton, Illinois, is one of two Registered Historic Places in DeWitt County. The other, the Magill House, was added to the Register in 2003. CH Moore Homestead DeWitt County Museum was added in 1979.

The New Zealand Superstock Championship is a dirt track racing competition held annually in New Zealand. Established in 1965, Superstocks is a full-contact motorsport where drivers strategically push competitors. The current Speedway New Zealand rules stipulate that cars must have engines no larger than 248 cubic inches, with most teams using V6 or V8 race engines. The estimated power output figure is around 500hp. Asher Rees won the most recent championship in 2021.
Giorgio Pisanò (1924–1997) was an Italian journalist, politician, and World War II veteran who served as a senator for MSI.
Roy Noble, OBE, DL, K.St.J, Rotary Fellow (born 1942) is a Welsh radio and television broadcaster, writer and Bevan Commissioner.

Derek Christopher Shepherd, M.D., F.A.C.S., also referred to as "McDreamy", is a fictional surgeon from the ABC medical drama Grey's Anatomy, portrayed by actor Patrick Dempsey. He made his first appearance in the pilot episode, "A Hard Day's Night", which was broadcast on March 27, 2005.

Derek was married to Addison Montgomery (Kate Walsh) before their divorce in 2007. Before his death in 2015, Derek was happily married to his longtime partner and wife Meredith Grey (Ellen Pompeo). The couple had three children together. Shepherd was formerly the Chief of Surgery at Seattle Grace Mercy West (now Grey Sloan Memorial Hospital) but abruptly resigned from his position as Chief in season 7 following the shooting.

Dempsey was nominated for the 2006 and 2007 Golden Globe for Best Performance by an Actor in a Television Series Drama for the role, and the 2006 SAG Award for Outstanding Performance by an Actor in a Drama Series.
HMS Penn was a P-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy during the Second World War.

A handscroll is a long, narrow scroll format in East Asian art. Originating in either India or China, handscrolls were used for texts and paintings, entering China by the 1st century CE. In China, bamboo or wooden slips were bound together for texts, evolving to paper and silk during the Han dynasty. By the Three Kingdoms period, it became a standard format for paintings. Introduced to Japan centuries later, Japanese horizontal picture scrolls are called emakimono. A handscroll includes a silk backing, frontispiece, artwork, and colophon, with a wooden stave and roller for support.
*Majha Hoshil Na* is an Indian Marathi-language drama and romance television series that aired on Zee Marathi from March 2, 2020, to August 28, 2021, and had 383 episodes.

Angela Dufresne (born 1969) is a Brooklyn based American artist known for paintings that explore narrative in a variety of ways. Dufresne holds a BFA from Kansas City Art Institute, MO and an MFA from Tyler School of Art, Philadelphia, PA. She is currently faculty at the Rhode Island School of Design.
F.C. Netanya is an Israeli football club based in Netanya, founded in 2012, and currently playing in Liga Gimel Sharon.

The ETTU Cup is the second most important continental tournament for clubs in European table tennis, after the European Champions League. The European Table Tennis Union (ETTU) has organized this cup since the 1964-65 season for men teams, and also for women teams a year later.

Boomplay or Boomplay Music is a media streaming and download service developed by Transsnet Music Limited. The service was first launched in Nigeria in 2015 by TECNO Mobile, Transsion Holdings. Boomplay has a freemium and subscription based service; basic features are free with advertisements or limitations, while additional features, such as download for offline play and ad-free listening are offered via paid subscriptions. The service is currently available for Web via www.boomplay.com, Android and iOS use. As of August 3, 2018, Boomplay Music recorded 10 million installations off the Google Play and App store. It currently has over 70 million monthly users (MAU) with a catalogue of over 85 million songs.

The Capture of Santiago was a military engagement that took place between 11 and 28 November 1585 during the newly declared Anglo-Spanish War. (Santiago is the largest island of the Cape Verde archipelago.) An English expedition led by Francis Drake captured the port town of Cidade Velha in the Cape Verde islands that had recently belonged to the Crown of Portugal. He sacked it and then marched inland before doing the same at São Domingos and Praia. Afterwards Drake left and continued his expedition to successfully raid the Spanish possessions in the Americas.

Sphinx (or Sphynx), was a French Sylphe-class brig launched at Genoa in 1813. She was handed over to naval suppliers at Genoa on 17 April 1814 when nearly completed as part-payment for debts. The next day the British occupied Genoa. Sphinx appears to have become the Royal Navy brig Regent, and then a Customs and Excise cruizer. Regent was sold in 1824, and then appeared as the Colombian government vessel Victoria, which is no longer traceable in online resources after 1828.

On July 9, 2014, a mass shooting occurred in a home located in northern Harris County, Texas, near the Spring census-designated place, a suburban area of the Greater Houston area, leaving six family members dead, four of them children, and a lone survivor. 34-year old Ronald Lee Haskell, was apprehended after a standoff that lasted several hours. Haskell was related to the victims via his ex-wife.
*Acacia tingoorensis*, or Tingoora wattle, is a tree endemic to the South Burnett Region of Queensland, Australia. It grows to 8 m high and blooms with golden flowers.
The Taylorcraft Auster was a British military liaison and observation aircraft produced by the Taylorcraft Aeroplanes (England) Limited company during the Second World War.

Nguyễn Tiến Trung (born 1983) is a Vietnamese cyber-dissident and political activist. While studying in France, he founded the Assembly of Vietnamese Youth for Democracy in 2006, advocating for political reform in Vietnam. Upon returning to Vietnam, he was arrested in 2009 and charged with plotting to overthrow the government. His arrest drew international condemnation, and he was eventually released in April 2014, serving the remaining three years under house arrest.
GE Power is a subsidiary of General Electric that serves the energy industry worldwide.

Down High School is a controlled co-educational grammar school located in Downpatrick, County Down, Northern Ireland. The school has students from the ages of eleven to eighteen in the senior school — of which there are approximately 970 pupils. On 21 July 2014, John O'Dowd, the education minister, approved a development proposal to close the preparatory department of Down High School and it closed on 30 June 2014. There are roughly 250 pupils engaged in A-level study in the sixth form. In July 2015, some £20 million was granted by the education minister to begin the new build project in 2017.

Susan Lynn Gordon (1949–2011) was an American child actress. She appeared in films such as *Attack of the Puppet People* and *The Five Pennies*, and in television series such as *The Twilight Zone* and *Gunsmoke*.

USS *Arizona* was a sidewheel steamer built in 1858. Seized by the Confederacy in 1862 and renamed *Caroline*, she was captured by the Union Navy and renamed *Arizona*. She was destroyed by fire on February 27, 1865.

Speshow is the fourth Mandarin studio album of Taiwanese Mandopop artist Show Lo (Chinese: 羅志祥). It was released on 17 November 2006 by Avex Taiwan. The name Speshow is a pun of 'Special' and his name 'Show', as he hoped it will be special for his fans. This is the last studio album of Show's, released by Avex Taiwan before his move to EMI Music Taiwan.

It was available on pre-order and four editions were released, which includes Speshow (Happiness Hunter MV Celebration Edition) (Speshow 幸福獵人影音慶功版), Speshow (Corner with Love Special Edition) (Speshow 愛*轉角 冠軍特別版) and Speshow (King's Game Live Gold Edition) (Speshow 國王遊戲LIVE黃金版) with 60 minutes of highlight live footage from Show Lo BenQ Speshow Concert at Taipei NanGang101.
The Harold Peary Show is a radio situation comedy broadcast in the United States September 17, 1950-June 13, 1951 on CBS. Some sources refer to the program as Honest Harold or The Hal Peary Show.
Martin Schütz (14 November 1963 – 25 February 2018) was a Swiss theoretical chemist and quantum chemist.

In various science/engineering applications, such as independent component analysis, image analysis, genetic analysis, speech recognition, manifold learning, and time delay estimation it is useful to estimate the differential entropy of a system or process, given some observations.

The simplest and most common approach uses histogram-based estimation, but other approaches have been developed and used, each with its own benefits and drawbacks. The main factor in choosing a method is often a trade-off between the bias and the variance of the estimate, although the nature of the (suspected) distribution of the data may also be a factor.
The Rafz train crash was a side collision between an Interregio train and an S-Bahn train on 20 February 2015, near Rafz, Switzerland. Six people were injured. The cause is under investigation.
HMS *Penn* (G77) was a P-class destroyer built for the Royal Navy during the Second World War.

"Just Got Started Lovin' You" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music artist James Otto. It was released in July 2007 as the lead single from his album *Sunset Man*. The song was written by Otto, Jim Femino, and D. Vincent Williams. John Rich, of Big & Rich, chose it as a single. A music video, directed by Ryan Smith, was filmed in November 2007.

Kris Fisher is a fictional character from the British Channel 4 soap opera *Hollyoaks*, portrayed by Gerard McCarthy. He was introduced as one of four new students in 2006 by Bryan Kirkwood, and remained in the series until 2010. Kris is known for his feisty personality and unique appearance, often experimenting with his style. Storylines have included a HIV scare, a bisexual love triangle, and his involvement in a carbon monoxide poisoning incident.

Entropy estimation is the process of estimating the entropy of a random variable from a set of samples. Several approaches exist, including histogram estimators, which approximate entropy using a histogram of observations, and methods based on sample-spacings, which estimate probability density based on the spacing between data points. Nearest-neighbor methods estimate entropy from the distribution of distances to each point's nearest neighbor. Bayesian estimators, like the NSB estimator, incorporate prior knowledge to improve estimation in under-sampled regimes. Another approach compares the expected entropy of a random sequence with the calculated entropy of the sample.
Joseph Biddle Wilkinson Jr. (February 20, 1845 – October 23, 1915) was a Member of the Board of General Appraisers.

The Ministry of War (Lang-de) was a ministry for military affairs of the Kingdom of Bavaria, founded as Ministerium des Kriegswesens on October 1, 1808 by King Maximilian I Joseph of Bavaria. It was located on the Ludwigstraße in Munich. Today the building, which was built by Leo von Klenze between 1824 and 1830, houses the Bavarian public record office, Bayerisches Hauptstaatsarchiv und Staatsarchiv München.

Matching logic is a formal logic mainly used to reason about the correctness of computer programs. Its operators use pattern matching to operate on the power set of states, rather than the set of states. It was created by Grigore Roșu and is used in the K Framework.

The University of Florida (UF) offers a distance education program in clinical toxicology, including a Master of Science (MS) degree and a graduate certificate. The programs, overseen by Dr. Ian Tebbett, are designed for health science professionals seeking advanced knowledge in toxicology. The MS program requires 32 credits, while the graduate certificate consists of 15 online credits. The certificate provides a background in the medical and toxicological principles of toxicants commonly encountered in poison control centers and emergency departments. A bachelor's degree in a health, health related or science related field is required before prospective students can enter the certificate program.

The Yale Glee Club is a mixed chorus at Yale University, founded in 1861. Comprising around 80 undergraduate and graduate students, the ensemble is led by Jeffrey Douma, the Marshall Bartholomew Professor of Choral Conducting at the Yale School of Music.

Originally an all-male group focused on glees, the Glee Club became a mixed chorus in 1970 after Yale College began admitting women. The group performs a wide range of choral works and undertakes annual domestic tours and international tours every two or three years. The Glee Club also runs an Emerging Composers Competition and the Fenno Heath Award.
Ukhra Assembly constituency was a legislative assembly constituency in West Bengal, India. Existing from 1969 to 2011, it was part of the Asansol Lok Sabha constituency.

Hulu Selangor is a district in Selangor, Malaysia, with its principal town being Kuala Kubu Bharu. It is governed by the Hulu Selangor Municipal Council and covers an area of 1,756.30 km2. As of 2020, the population was 239,978. The district's economy is driven by manufacturing and tourism, with a focus on attracting industrial investment and leveraging its natural scenery.

The Ministry of Health (Lang-tr) is the ministry of the Government of Turkey responsible for proposing and executing the government policy on health, planning and providing healthcare and protecting consumers. Likewise, it is responsible for proposing and executing the government policy on social cohesion and inclusion, family, protection of minors, youth and of care for dependent or disabled persons. The Ministry is headquartered in the Bakanlıklar in Ankara.

The Ministry of Health is headed by the Minister of Health, who is appointed by the President of Turkey at request of the Turkish Parliament. The current minister is Fahrettin Koca, serving since 10 July 2018.

Woodford Island is an inland island in the Far-North Coast of New South Wales, Australia. It is formed by the Clarence River where it splits into the South Arm and North Arm at the small village of Brushgrove then reforms at the town of Maclean. The island is the largest in NSW, either inland or offshore, at 37 km2.

A mobile (UK: /ˈmoʊbaɪl/,US: /ˈmoʊbiːl/) is a type of kinetic sculpture constructed to take advantage of the principle of equilibrium. It consists of a number of rods, from which weighted objects or further rods hang. The objects hanging from the rods balance each other, so that the rods remain more or less horizontal. Each rod hangs from only one string, which gives it the freedom to rotate about the string. An ensemble of these balanced parts hang freely in space, by design without coming into contact with each other.

Mobiles are popular in the nursery, where they hang over cribs to give infants entertainment and visual stimulation. Mobiles have inspired many composers, including Morton Feldman and Earle Brown who were inspired by Alexander Calder's mobiles to create mobile-like indeterminate pieces. John Cage wrote the music for the short film Works of Calder that focused on Calder's mobiles. Frank Zappa stated that his compositions employ a principle of balance similar to Calder mobiles.

Henri Murger (born and died in Paris, 1822 – 1861) was a French novelist and poet. Born to immigrant parents, Murger had a fragmented education and worked various jobs before beginning his literary career around 1841. Early works included literary and poetic essays, and he later edited fashion and millinery trade papers.

Murger's breakthrough came with *Scènes de la vie de bohème*, a novel that achieved significant success. He followed it with a sequel, *Scènes de la vie de jeunesse*, in 1851, though subsequent works did not reach the same level of popularity. Despite receiving the Légion d'honneur in 1859, Murger faced financial difficulties and health issues throughout his life. He died in poverty at the age of 38. The French government funded his funeral, which was attended by many figures from journalism, literature, theatre, and the arts. A fund was started by *Le Figaro* to raise money for his monument.
